speaker: interviewer
text: Today's June 23rd, 1995. Interview is with Ben Gerstein. The interviewer is Alison Smith. It will be done in Toronto, Ontario. And it will be done in English. Today is June 23rd, 1995. The interview is with Ben Gerstein-- G-E-R-S-T-E-I-N. Interviewer is me, Alison Smith. It's A-L-I-S-O-N S-M-I-T-H. It will be done in Toronto, Ontario in English. Good afternoon. What is your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben Gerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the last name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. G-E-R-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the name you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the name I was born with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben. Benzion. I've been called Ben since I've been a kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Benzion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben-- B-E-N-Z-I-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk-- L-U-C-K I believe, Poland, which is now the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you situate that for me in--?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's sort of a small province. It's Wolyn. It's called Wolyn-- W-O-L-Y-N. The capital of Wolyn was Rovno-- R-O-N-O-V I believe, and Lutsk was the second city, sort of, to that province-- which is now, as I say, it's-- it's in Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you born on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- I was actually born February the 4th, 1931. When I say actually, my papers got mixed up and I'm January 11th, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents'--

speaker: interviewee
text: Made me a little older.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Beila-- B-E-I-L-A. And Shia. Shia is the-- is a name taken from Joshua, or Shia as he was called-- is S-H-I-A Gerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's Shia Gerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a grain merchant-- buying grain from farmers, and the stock market, and shipping it all over Europe, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: A housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your address was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was 18 Knidawska. Knidawska-- you want to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh boy. I wish you didn't ask me that. It's K-N-I-D-A-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe the house you grew up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a large house. It had two huge flats underneath. It's like almost a three-story house. It was on a hill. Part of it was basement, which they turned into retail stores-- retail shops. There were two huge apartments on the second floor which, as we called it, the main floor. And then we had some small little apartment houses above which were rented out to tenants. And we lived on one half of the house. And we rented it, again-- again the other half of that main floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Relatively speaking, for those days, quite well, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters? We had one-- I have one sister, Myrna-- M-Y-R-N-A, called Musia, in Poland-- M-U-C-- M-U-S-I-A. And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in '28. 1928. I believe it was summer of 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did. It was like a kindergarten school, a Jewish type of organization, kindergarten school for a couple of years. And then I was in public school for about three years-- two years-- two to three years. Then the war broke out. In a regular Polish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your favorite subjects in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Playing soccer. [LAUGHING] I don't think there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that far back, honestly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to be a race car driver. Would you believe? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you think you were going to become a race car driver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I always liked cars. And to me, it was the most magical thing. I could see we had a few cars even then. As a matter of fact, when we were liberated, I didn't have a car, but I had two motorcycles. So it was a start. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship with the-- with the other-- with your fellow students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Yeah, very good. Always a happy child, and lots of playing. And just having a normal childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh sure. Quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. Quite a few. We had to go to school. Our school was-- we lived like in a little suburb. Can you picture, I don't know, miniature Lake Ontario. And we were divided-- we were on the island, let's assume-- you know, like central island here. And they built a-- like a stone, a roadway. And they built two bridges so the water could get around it. We lived on that outside of that city. And the school was in the city. And we had to always drive forth and back-- or walk or bicycle-- depends on the weather. It was seasonal like here. And more than not, there wasn't probably a week or day went by that we didn't get into some kind of a fight with the gentile kids calling us name and Jews. And all kinds of stuff. And you learn how to fight. [CHUCKLES] One good thing came out of it-- you learned how to handle yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your city very segregated with Jewish life and gentile life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty well. Oh yes. Yet there were some gentile people living right within-- within our little hamlet, which was 90% Jews. But there were 10% gentiles-- of mixed-- of Polish, Czechoslovakian, some Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your Jewish upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing religious. My father was never that religious at all. But he was observant-- did his duties, we went to shul, and did his-- it was always a kosher home. And-- and, you know, I tried to-- he tried to bring up the same way, but never pushing religion on me, as such, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were Friday nights spent in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were spent on a typical Jewish home Friday night. They were doing the candles and the certain dinners that we have Friday. And the family get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite celebration in the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I enjoyed Passover. Purim was always fun. That's a fun holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there big family gatherings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly big, yeah. Fairly big.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first-- your first memory of-- of Germany, and the goings on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young to get involved with-- with what's going on Germany, except when the refugees that came-- they came over to our city-- our part of the city. They ran away from that German occupation half of Poland. So they ran over, and they took up residence as refugees in our area. That was under the Russian part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just the news that they brought us-- that there were some pogroms and Jewish off-and-on. They're used to pogroms. And not that they liked it, but they were used to it. It was nothing new. And the Kristallnacht, we heard about that. Not in detail, but almost like second, third-hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I heard about-- again, I was nine years-- eight years old. So you do-- you're involved with your own childhood stuff. When I found out is-- is when the Germans came. That's 1941. When they invaded our part of Poland. That's when I realized the severity of the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents political?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of literature you had in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- yeah, my father was involved-- being in business, and quite heavy into it-- we read just, how shall we say, at that time, contemporary stuff from poets, Jewish poets and stuff like that. But nothing political, Zionism, as far as that. And Betar, and all that type of stuff. We were never really that much into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite author or favorite book?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I liked listening to stories. [CHUCKLING] With our rabbi telling us the history of-- of our religion, and, you know, Solomon and the King and all that. But I'm talking about childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exact-- at eight years-- at 1939, the Russians came in, and it was a whole-- a complete change of life for me. See, all of a sudden you see a new occupation. And they arrested my father for nine months-- the Communists did, the Russians, because he was a capitalist, you see? Which meant a rich man, a capitalist. So he was in eight, nine months in jail. Until the-- and they tried to get the witnesses-- any Ukrainian or Polish man, anybody-- a gentile-- coming forward to put the finger on him, so to speak. Nobody came forward. So they released him. And where was he in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the Lutsk-- from Lutsk, from [INAUDIBLE] there was a huge prison there. That's where they kept him-- not tortured him or anything, but that's where they kept him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- Did your sister have the same sort of lifestyle as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty well. Being three four years older, I'm sure she remembers more than-- than I do of her life, of what's happened. I remember more-- the extra year, the extra two years means quiet a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the same school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the public school for a while, for a couple of years. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your first-- your first memory of the war of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was not much of a fight when the Russians occupied that part of Poland. I don't know why. I don't remember that that well. But I do remember very well when the Germans-- when the First World War started, which was in 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember being woken up at 6 o'clock in the morning with bombs, with explosions. We didn't know what they were. Explosions. And we had some Russian boarders from the Russian Army that occupied Poland. They rented-- they didn't take it on force-- but they rented a couple of rooms from upstairs from our apartment above. And I remember that very well. And we all got woken up-- 7 o'clock, 6:30-7:00. We all went out on the balcony. And the Russian soldiers came up with pajamas. And he looks around, says, oh, there's just maneuvers. Don't worry about it. It's just maneuvers. And my father looks at him. He says, I don't think these are maneuvers. He says, I think we have a war. So he says, no way. [INAUDIBLE] We have a pact with Hitler. He says, you better look at the bumps, not the pact. Those are the words I remember. And then we saw black smoke coming from the city part. And somehow the planes never came to bomb our part. It was strictly a-- a no military thing at all, where we lived. So all the-- everything started basically in the city. And within-- I don't know-- four weeks, five weeks, the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just getting back for a minute, between 1939 and 1941, were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewer
text: --lots of refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there lots of refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. There were quite a few refugees that ran away from the German occupation. And as a matter of fact, some of them stayed-- not immediately in our rooms-- but they stayed in our house in the apartments above that became available. Houses to almost everyone, took somebody in. And they lived on handouts basically. They had jobs or anything. So we fed then, we gave them food, we gave them to eat, and-- and stuff like that. And most of them had their homes back on the German occupation of Poland. And they just got sick and tired of living on handouts. The decided to register to go back. The Russian government asked them fill out a form. Anybody that wants to go back to the German occupation, fill out this form. I'd say about 90% of the people filled out the form to go back. I mean, they had their businesses there, their bank accounts, their money, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and it wasn't that bad in that year and half, two years, you see? So they figure, ah, maybe they should go back before everything is ruined, and the business is lost. And maybe the war will be over and, you know, make peace or something. Well, they registered to go back all right. At 1 o'clock in the morning-- between 1:00 and 3:00 in the morning-- we got woken up by trucks and trains and you name it. And they took every one of those registered people to go back to Germany. They loaded them up on trucks. They put them into cars, cattle cars basically-- the Russians did. And they sent them to Siberia as punishment. That means, you want to go to Germany? You deserve Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as it turned out, these were the lucky ones. Nine out of ten survived in Siberia, while the ones that were left over, it was the other way around-- eight of ten-- eight out of a hundred survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the town? Did they think they were lucky?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not. We didn't think so. I mean, we could have run away. Some of us did. When they saw that-- when they saw that Germany is declaring war-- not declaring war. They just started a war with Russia, some of-- of our people did run away. But a minority-- very little. You just don't leave and leave a house and business and everything, and just run off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever talk about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have relatives or family in other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I have?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point? Sure. We had-- my father had two brothers. My mother had one brother in Canada. So we kept in touch with them. If we wanted to probably-- but just-- that's before the Russias came in, or before the Germans came in. By that time it was too late anyhow. You see, once the Russians invaded Poland, we didn't have much of a chance to get out. When the Germans invaded, well for sure we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of them urging you to come over to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know what was happening, only what we wrote them. If we wanted to, we would have written to them. Can you send us papers? They would have. But you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that was thinking of converting religions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Jews converting to-- no. Only the assimilated Jews, you know. They for sure felt sure that nothing's going to happen to them-- married to gentile girls, or girls-- or Jewish girls married to gentle men, you know. They didn't think, oh well, nothing's going to happen to them. Little did they know, but they went back three generations. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So between 1939 and 1941, would you say your lifestyle did not really change very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that much. OK, the Russias-- but then my father was in jail. I was very sad. But I was still young. I was still 19 years old. Nobody stopped me from doing anything. We still did our games, and our school, and our fun, and skiing, and, you know, and fighting, and the usual age. And-- and we still were, relatively speaking, well-off, even though there was no business, but we were well-off. There was no shortage of food or anything, or clothes or anything. Just my sister and I-- we were a small family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, did you think any of the happenings in Germany would come to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally never gave it a thought. I'm sure my father thought, but who would think that Germany would invade that part of Poland-- declare war on-- on Poland. Russia surely didn't think about it. You see? So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after that time in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When the Germans invaded, they came in. And the front army came in. They right away, there was-- there was-- they asked for laborers. They needed laborers to dig bunkers. So they-- they took 300 laborers, a [? hundred ?] [? lived ?] [? there. ?] They right away, they went into the Jewish community, took people that run the communities. And they said, look, we need 500 people to-- before we come in with the machine guns, you better send 500 men to the-- help dig ditches, and stuff like that. And my father says, I'm not going. When they went-- when the committee came in to look for him, so my mother says we haven't seen him. He's somewhere in a farm doing business with some grain people. We haven't seen him for weeks. He was hiding out, because he just didn't trust the Germans. Those 500 people never came back. They were automatically taken to the forest and shot. You see, that was the initial introduction into the whole scenery. And five months later, again, the same situation. Never seen again. Inside our-- our city, all the [INAUDIBLE] they beat up a few Jews-- the Germans did. Or the Ukrainians, the [INAUDIBLE]. But nothing on a large scale. So we were relatively safe from--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, any German can come-- come into our apartment, and take out a gun and kill somebody. And that's it. Just walk away. Which they did later on, when the SS came in. I don't know if you're familiar with the-- with that-- those regiments. The front army wasn't that bad. They had their own problems with the front-- with the Russian front, they had. Pardon? Where did your father hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, would you believe it? With some gentile people renting from us up above. And then he came out. He came back sort of speaking from the-- from the trip that he took. He came back. And that's it. And then he would hide out again, you know. Because they needed the men, you see-- or not needed. They wanted to eliminate as many fighting people, or capable of fighting, as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what age and up were they looking for? You were too young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were too young to go? What age were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They would probably pick people 16 and over, or 18, that type of thing, you see. As a matter of fact, a year later he was working for a German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a general-- a low rank general I believe. And he-- [INAUDIBLE] that was like more of a protection. See, I'm working for a German. And he was working for them. He got along well, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was helping just maintain, you know, like an outpost type of thing. You know, clearing, and the water, they purifying water, and stuff. He knew things like that. He was very good in agricultural and all that, you know. So he knew all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told him how to do it. He was very surprised. And he kept him on as [INAUDIBLE]. He took out-- that same general-- took out a gun when he was drunk-- took out a gun and told my father to shoot that Jew there. There was another guy working with him. So my father took out the gun and shot. And told the-- the other guy to run. He came back and said, what did you do with him? He says, I threw him in the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yet not-- when the drunkenness went, he was very nice. Like a [INAUDIBLE]. There were quite a few incidents like that. Quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know anybody that did go away as a worker and never came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Sure. Never heard them. Never heard them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time you were still going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. 1941 I stopped going to school. When the Germans invaded us, we are watching ourselves. There was always rumors that they liquidated this town, that they're sending people to concentration camps. But you were so isolated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were rumors, you see? Well, yes. We know. But who ran away? Who? You see, you couldn't find a Jew anywhere, other than in that immediate area. You weren't allowed to leave that city, even though it was not a ghetto-- a ghetto with borders. But we were designated in an area within blocks, and that's where we had to stay. You see, outside of there, you could be shot. Anybody-- doesn't matter. Didn't have to be a German-- Ukrainian, Polack, anybody. So what was your day-to-day life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too bad. I knew-- I learned how to be scared. When you saw a German truck run up, or-- or cars with some militia, I ran right away for-- for a hiding place. I didn't run home. I ran-- always on my mind, to run away to hide. I don't know what's going to happen later, but hide. I remember one incident. There was couple of shots. And I saw Germans with a truck. And I said, well that's it. I ran up a tree. I hid in a tree. I figured if I don't see them, they don't see me. You know, that type of thing. And I was there for about an hour, until I saw it was OK. Than I climbed down. A few times that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were a lot of rumors. But it's going to be next week. It's going to be five days later. It's going to be next month. So you got tired of hiding, out of that quick hiding thing. Because where do you run, first of all? You see? You see what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt-- she's still alive here-- my mother's sister-- she was working for a Ukrainian. It's best if you could work for somebody. You see, then you're busy. You were not designated to take away. You were busy. So she worked for this Ukrainian family. He was in the Ukrainian militia, working for the Germans. She had a gun. And he liked her. And he said to her, Jenny you better-- there's something I don't like, something is going to happen. They told us to check our rifles. They told us a certain day. And there's no reason, unless they are thinking of-- of something serious this time. I don't think it's a rumor. I'll let you know what else I can find out. But be on your guard. And then about three days later, he says, I think this is it. You better run. Tell your immediate family, run. I don't care where they run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my aunt told her parents. But all the people-- where do you run? You see what I'm saying? Where do you hide? There's no forests. See, if there were-- if there were forests immediately, you can run into the forest and hide. You had no place to run, because every Ukrainian had the right to kill you. They-- they thought all the Jews had the money. So naturally we're walking around with diamonds in your pocket. So there was really no place to run, unless you made certain arrangements, or somehow like [? red ?] dug a hole in the floor or something like that. Then you had a chance. You had a chance-- the immediate hiding place. My father was starting to think seriously. Once she told-- well he was thinking about that before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father also had a gun, which was completely-- I mean, he would-- if they ever find one, the whole family's gone. But he figured, what have I got to lose? Because at least if they come here, he says, I'll kill one German, at least for sure. And that would give him the satisfaction. In the meantime, he was finding-- and he found a place near Lawrow. It's just outside of Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a little-- a halfway camp for German. It was on a main route. And when a German battalion came by, they had to be fed and all. That would be the [? bundes ?] station where they would stop. And it was like a little village. And they were doing bricks-- making bricks and stuff like that. And-- and they had, I don't know, about 30 Germans with a cook. You know, a regular. And they were staying. And they heard that they are not bad. That they're looking for workers. They don't care who they are. You know, and it didn't make sense, but it made-- somehow it worked. There were Jews working there, and this guy was not bad at all-- the guy that ran the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father found out about that. He got a hold of a Ukrainian or Polish man. I forget now. And he asked him. He'll pay him and all that, for the food and everything. They were like friends. They used to deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if something happens in the city, if the liquidation comes in, if we have a chance to run away, he'll send me, my sister, and my mother over until the liquidation's over. And then we look further for a place to hide. And he agreed. So we waited until the last minute. And then my aunt came over and she says, yeah. I think this is it. You better run.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop tape one right here.
